content:
I am currently a PhD Candidate in Economics at the University of Iowa. I will be on the job-market during the 2021-2022 academic year. My research interests include econometrics, applied econometrics, and empirical industrial organization. In particular, I am interested in semi-parametric and non-parametric models with endogeneity and unobserved heterogeneity. I am interested in applying these econometric techniques to the literature on firm productivity heterogeneity and auctions.
My job market paper applies nonparametric identification results to firm production functions where productivity has heterogeneous effects on output and input demand functions. My estimates suggest there is substantial variation across firms which corresponds to the rank in the distribution of output and input demand.

title: Justin Doty

summary:
A Dynamic Framework for Identification and Estimation of Nonseparable Production Functions by Justin Doty (2021)
Appendix C.2: Labor Dynamics Figure 17: Impulse Response of Adjustment Shocks to Labor This plot reports the difference in labor demand between firms hit with low, medium-low, medium-high and high labor shocks and medium labor shocks at different levels labor demand. The first row corresponds to firms hit by low labor shocks at year 2.

A Dynamic Framework for Identification and Estimation of Nonseparable Production Functions by Justin Doty (2021)
Empirical Application with Interactive Plots Figure 1: Output Elasticities Panel (a) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to capital evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of capital averaged over values of labor and materials that correspond to the percentiles of capital.
Panel (b) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to labor evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of labor averaged over values of capital and materials that correspond to the percentiles of labor.

A Dynamic Framework for Identification and Estimation of Nonseparable Production Functions by Justin Doty (2021)
Appendix C.3: R&D Activities Figure 18: Productivity Persistence for Non-performing and R&D Performing Firms Panel (a) is the estimate of the productivity persistence for non-R&D firms evaluated at percentiles of innovation shocks and percentiles of previous productivity levels.
Panel (b) is the estimate of the productivity persistence for R&D firms evaluated at percentiles of innovation shocks and percentiles of previous productivity levels averaged over R&D.

A Dynamic Framework for Identification and Estimation of Nonseparable Production Functions by Justin Doty (2021)
Appendix C.4: Correcting for Selection Bias Figure 25: Output Elasticities Panel (a) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to capital evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of capital averaged over values of labor and materials that correspond to the percentiles of capital.
Panel (b) is the estimate of the output elasticity with respect to labor evaluated at percentiles of the conditional output distribution and percentiles of labor averaged over values of capital and materials that correspond to the percentiles of labor.
